title: Jam Roly-Poly

overview.description: A traditional steamed suet pastry rolled around raspberry or blackberry jam and served hot with custard.

method: Grease a large sheet of baking parchment.
Place the flour, suet, sugar and salt in a large bowl.
Gradually add the milk and mix until a soft dough forms.
Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface.
Roll it into a rectangle approximately 25cm by 35cm.
Spread the jam over the dough, leaving a 2cm border around the edges.
Roll the dough up from one of the shorter ends into a thick log.
Pinch the seam gently to seal it.
Place the pudding seam-side down on the greased baking parchment.
Wrap the pudding loosely in the parchment, leaving room for expansion.
Wrap the parchment parcel in foil, sealing the ends securely.
Place the parcel on a rack inside a large saucepan or steamer.
Add boiling water beneath the rack.
Cover and steam for approximately 2 hours.
Check the water level during cooking and top up with boiling water if necessary.
Slice thickly and serve immediately with hot custard.

learning.did-you-know: Jam roly-poly is sometimes affectionately known as 'dead man's arm' because of its rolled shape and traditional wrapping.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make a suet pastry and how steaming allows a rolled pastry pudding to cook gently while remaining soft and tender.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A classic old-fashioned British pudding that is inexpensive, filling and ideal for a traditional Saturday pudding rotation.
chapman-tips: Don't roll the pastry too thin or the pudding may become difficult to handle.
Leave a border around the edge so jam does not escape excessively during rolling.
Use a firm jam rather than a very runny preserve.
Blackberry jam would work particularly well with your homemade lower-sugar version.
Don't wrap the pudding too tightly; the pastry needs room to expand.
Keep the water at a steady simmer during steaming.
Make sure the water does not come into direct contact with the pudding.
Serve immediately while the suet pastry is soft and warm.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Cool completely, wrap securely and freeze in portions.
notes.leftovers: Reheat by steaming or gently in the microwave and serve with custard.
